lesson.title: Two Pis, one cable: can they just talk?
lesson.summary: A guided lab that shows the difference between link, frames, and reachability by connecting two nodes with one Ethernet cable.

hardware.description: Drive two Raspberry Pi nodes over SSH while their eth0 ports are connected by one cable.

virtual.description: Recreate the two-node cable with Linux network namespaces and a veth pair.
limitations: No physical carrier, speed, duplex, or link lights.
No real Raspberry Pi MAC vendor prefix.
Link-up chatter differs from physical hardware.

read_only.description: Teach from the README, diary, and manifest when hardware or Linux namespaces are unavailable.

agent_defaults.safe_first_step: Read README.md, diaries/00_two-pis-one-cable.md, and lessons/00/README.md before running scripts.
ask_before: Building or flashing Raspberry Pi images.
Running disk-writing commands such as dd.

recovery.situation: Virtual lab interrupted or left behind.
Hardware lesson should return eth0 to a blank wire.

beats.question: Is there even a wire?
beats.human_action: Unplug and then seat the Ethernet cable.
expected_observations: Before link-up, eth0 shows NO-CARRIER and state DOWN.
After link-up, eth0 shows LOWER_UP and state UP.
ethtool reports Link detected: yes, speed, and duplex after the cable is seated.
common_failures: The virtual lab skips this beat because veth has no physical layer.
If LOWER_UP never appears, check both cable ends, power, and the interface name.

beats.question: Are frames flowing before we configure anything?
beats.concept: Layer 2: link-up chatter, broadcast, multicast, and local discovery.
expected_observations: tcpdump or tshark shows frames shortly after the link bounces.
IPv6 neighbor solicitation and multicast listener reports may appear.
Real Pis may also show mDNS hostname announcements and DHCP discovery.
A naive ping to 10.10.0.2 still fails.
common_failures: If tshark is missing, scripts fall back to tcpdump output.
If no frames appear, confirm both ends of eth0 are up.

beats.question: Where did the failed ping go?
beats.concept: Layer 3 identity: a live wire is not automatically a route.
expected_observations: ip -4 addr show eth0 has no inet line.
ip route get 10.10.0.2 does not resolve to dev eth0.
On hardware, the route may point at wlan0; in the bare virtual lab, it may be unreachable.
common_failures: If eth0 already has 10.10.0.x, run reset or recreate the virtual lab.

beats.question: What changes when the wire has IPv4 identities?
beats.concept: Assigning addresses creates a connected route on eth0.
expected_observations: ip route get to the peer now resolves to dev eth0.
The source address matches the node's assigned 10.10.0.x address.
common_failures: NetworkManager may reject duplicate connection names if the lesson was already run; use scripts/reset.sh on hardware or recreate the virtual lab.

beats.question: How does an IP address become a frame on the wire?
beats.concept: ARP maps the peer IPv4 address to its MAC address, then the ICMP echo can leave.
expected_observations: After the neighbor cache is flushed, capture shows who-has 10.10.0.2 tell 10.10.0.1.
The peer replies with an is-at MAC address.
The second ICMP echo skips ARP because the neighbor cache is warm.
common_failures: If tcpdump cannot capture, check root privileges on the target node.
If ping fails after addressing, verify both nodes have eth0 addresses in 10.10.0.0/24.
